title: Finding derivative with fundamental theorem of calculus
description: Fundamental theorem of calculus practice problems
topic_path: The fundamental theorem of calculus and accumulation functions
Finding derivative with fundamental theorem of calculus

hints:
## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{2t + 7}$ is continuous on $[1,9]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{1}^{x}\sqrt{2t+7}\,dt$

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{2x+7}$

## Evaluating $g'(9)$

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(9) = \sqrt{2\cdot9+7}=\sqrt{25}=5$

The answer:

$g'(9)=5$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{19-t}$ is continuous on $[1,3]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{1}^x\sqrt{19 - t}\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{19 - x}$

## Evaluating $g'(3)$

$\displaystyle g'(3) = \sqrt{19 - 3}=\sqrt{16}=4$

The answer:

$g'(3)=4$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{t^2 + 11}$ is continuous on $[-10,-5]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-10}^x\sqrt{t^2 + 11}\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{x^2 + 11}$

## Evaluating $g'(-5)$

$\displaystyle g'(-5) = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + 11}=\sqrt{36}=6$

The answer:

$g'(-5)=6$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{10t + 1}$ is continuous on $[0,8]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{0}^x\sqrt{10t + 1}\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{10x  + 1}$

## Evaluating $g'(8)$

$\displaystyle g'(8) = \sqrt{10(8) + 1}=\sqrt{81}=9$

The answer:

$g'(8)=9$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{5+4\cos {t}}$ is continuous on $[0,\pi]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{\,0}^{\,x}\sqrt{5+4\cos{t}}\,dt $

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{5+4\cos{x}}$

## Evaluating $g'(\pi)$

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(\pi) = \sqrt{5+4\cdot \cos{\pi}}=\sqrt{5+4\cdot (-1)}=\sqrt{5-4}=1$

The answer:

$g'(\pi)=1$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sqrt{5+4\tan {t}}$ is continuous on $[0,\dfrac{\pi}{4}]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{\,1}^{\,x}\sqrt{5+4\tan{t}}\,dt $

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sqrt{5+4\tan{x}}$

## Evaluating $g'\left(\dfrac\pi4\right)$

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime\Big(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\Big)= \sqrt{5+4\cdot \tan({\pi}/4)}=\sqrt{5+4\cdot 1}=\sqrt{9}=3$

The answer:

$g'\left(\dfrac\pi4\right)=3$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sin(t) $ is continuous on $[-\pi,\pi]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-\pi}^{x}\sin(t)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\sin(x)$

## Evaluating $g'(\pi)$

$\displaystyle g'(\pi)= \sin(\pi) = 0$

The answer:

$g'(\pi)=0$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \sin(t) $ is continuous on $[-\pi,\pi]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-\pi}^{x}\cos(t)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\cos(x)$

## Evaluating $g'(\pi)$

$\displaystyle g'(\pi)= \cos(\pi) = -1$

The answer:

$g'(\pi)=-1$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \dfrac{13}{{1 + {t^{\,2}}}}$ is continuous on $[2,5]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{2}^{x} {\frac{13}{{1 + {t^{\,2}}}}\,dt} $

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) = \frac{13}{{1 + {x^{\,2}}}} $

## Evaluating $g'(5)$

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(5) =\frac{13}{{1 + {x^{\,2}}}}= \frac{13}{{1 + 5^2}}=\dfrac{13}{26}=\dfrac12 $

The answer:

$g'(5)=\dfrac{1}{2}$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{{1 + {t^{\,3}}}}$ is continuous on $[2,2]$.
## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{2}^{x} {\frac{1}{{1 + {t^{\,3}}}}\,dt} $

So the theorem tells us:

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) = \frac{1}{{1 + {x^{\,3}}}} $
## Evaluating $g'\left(2\right)$

$\displaystyle g\,^\prime(2) = \frac{1}{{1 + 2^3}}=\dfrac{1}{9}$

The answer:

$g'\left(2\right)=\dfrac19$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = \dfrac{t^2 - t}{\sqrt{t}}$ is continuous on $[0,4]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{0}^{x}\dfrac{t^2 - t}{\sqrt{t}}\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =\dfrac{x^2 - x}{\sqrt{x}} $

## Evaluating $g'(4)$

$\displaystyle g'(4)= \dfrac{4^2 - 4}{\sqrt{4}} = \dfrac{12}{2} = 6$

The answer:

$g'(4)=6$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = 5t^2 -t$ is continuous on $[0,2]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{0}^{x}(5t^2 - t)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =5x^2 - x$

## Evaluating $g'(2)$

$\displaystyle g'(2)= 5(2)^2 - 2 = 18$

The answer:

$g'(2)=18$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = t - t^2$ is continuous on $[-7,10]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-7}^{x}(t - t^2)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =x - x^2 $

## Evaluating $g'(10)$

$\displaystyle g'(10)= 10 - 10^2 = -90$

The answer:

$g'(10)=-90$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = 3t^2 + 4t$ is continuous on $[1,2]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{1}^{x}(3t^2 +4t )\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =3x^2  +4x $

## Evaluating $g'(2)$

$\displaystyle g'(2)= 3(2)^2 + 4(2) = 12 + 8 = 20$

The answer:

$g'(2)=20$

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =10x + 2$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = -2t  +4$ is continuous on $[-8,-3]$.

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-8}^{x}(-2t + 4)\,dt\,$

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = 8 - t$ is continuous on $[-1,1]$.

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-1}^{x}(8 - t)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =8 - x$

$\displaystyle g'(1)= 8 - 1 = 7$

## The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

If $~\displaystyle g(x)=\int_a^xf(t)\,dt\,$, then $~g^\prime (x)=f(x)\,$

This only works if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$.

Thankfully, the function $f(t) = 10 - 3t$ is continuous on $[-10,-4]$.

## Applying the theorem

We're given:

$\displaystyle g(x) = \int_{-10}^{x}(10 - 3t)\,dt\,$

So the theorem tells us:

$ \displaystyle g\,^\prime(x) =10 - 3x$

## Evaluating $g'(-4)$

$\displaystyle g'(-4)= 10 - 3(-4) = 22$

The answer:

$g'(-4)=22$
